mission.title: Install and Configure Linode Kubernetes Engine (LKE)
mission.description: Linode Kubernetes Engine (LKE) is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies the deployment and management of Kubernetes clusters on Linode's infrastructure. It is ideal for users who want to leverage Linode's cloud capabilities without the overhead of managing the control plane. Key trade-offs include limited customization compared to self-managed clusters and potential costs associated with cloud resources.

steps.title: Install the Linode CLI
steps.description:
The Linode CLI is required to interact with the Linode API for creating and managing your Kubernetes cluster.

```bash

curl -O https://linode.com/cli/linode-cli-1.23.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz

tar -xzf linode-cli-1.23.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz

sudo mv linode-cli /usr/local/bin/

linode-cli configure

```

> Download the Linode CLI binary.
> Extract the downloaded tarball.
> Move the CLI to a directory in your PATH.
> Configure the CLI with your Linode API token.

steps.title: Create a Kubernetes Cluster
steps.description:
This command creates a new Kubernetes cluster on Linode with the specified version and node count.

```bash

linode-cli k8s cluster-create --label my-k8s-cluster --k8s-version 1.30 --node-count 3 --region us-east

```

> Replace 'my-k8s-cluster' with your desired cluster name.

steps.title: Set Up Kubeconfig
steps.description:
This step configures your kubeconfig file to connect to the newly created Kubernetes cluster.

```bash

linode-cli k8s kubeconfig-view my-k8s-cluster --format yaml > ~/.kube/config

```

> This command saves the kubeconfig to your local machine.

steps.title: Verify Cluster Nodes
steps.description:
After the cluster is created, this command checks the status of the nodes to ensure they are ready.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see the nodes listed with a status of 'Ready'.

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
Configure autoscaling and monitoring for your cluster to ensure optimal performance.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/linode/linode-cloud-controller-manager/v0.9.5/deploy/ccm-linode-template.yaml

```

> This applies the necessary configurations for the Linode Cloud Controller Manager.

uninstall.title: Delete the Kubernetes Cluster
uninstall.description:
This command will remove the Kubernetes cluster and all associated resources. Be aware that this will result in data loss.

```bash

linode-cli k8s cluster-delete my-k8s-cluster

```

> Replace 'my-k8s-cluster' with your actual cluster name.

uninstall.title: Clean Up Orphaned Resources
uninstall.description:
Ensure that any orphaned resources such as load balancers and persistent volumes are also removed.

```bash

linode-cli nodebalancer delete --label my-nodebalancer

```

> Replace 'my-nodebalancer' with your actual NodeBalancer name.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Kubernetes Cluster
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your cluster data. Then, upgrade the cluster version and verify the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl get pods --all-namespaces

linode-cli k8s cluster-update my-k8s-cluster --k8s-version 1.30

kubectl get nodes

```

> Check the status of all pods before proceeding.
> Upgrade to the specified Kubernetes version.
> Verify that all nodes are in 'Ready' status after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually indicates that the kubeconfig is not set up correctly or the cluster is not reachable.

**Fix:**
Re-run the kubeconfig setup command: 'linode-cli k8s kubeconfig-view my-k8s-cluster --format yaml > ~/.kube/config'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'No resources found in default namespace'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that there are no resources deployed in the default namespace.

**Fix:**
Check if you are in the correct namespace or deploy resources using 'kubectl apply -f <resource-file>'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create service: Service is invalid'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the service configuration is incorrect or missing required fields.

**Fix:**
Review the service YAML file for correctness and ensure all required fields are present.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient CPU resources'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the cluster does not have enough resources to schedule the requested pods.

**Fix:**
Scale up the cluster by adding more nodes or upgrading existing nodes.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new scheduling features and improved resource management.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the use of legacy API versions for certain resources.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.29, ensure that all custom resources are compatible with the new API versions.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get nodes' showing all nodes in 'Ready' status, confirming that your cluster is operational.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the necessary tools installed and a valid Linode API token.
